data.description:
This monthly article reports sector metrics in the S&P 500 Index, aiming at a top-down analysis based on value, quality and momentum. It may also help analyze ETFs tracking this index. Among them, iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV) is second behind SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) for dollar volume and third behind Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO) and SPY for assets under management. IVV has a lower expense ratio than SPY: only 0.03%.

Shortcut

The next two paragraphs in italic describe the dashboard methodology. They are necessary for new readers to understand the metrics. If you are used to this series or if you are short of time, you can skip them and go to the charts.

Base Metrics

I calculate the median value of five fundamental ratios in every sector: Earnings Yield ("EY"), Sales Yield ("SY"), Free Cash Flow Yield ("FY"), Return on Equity ("ROE"), Gross Margin ("GM"). All are calculated on trailing 12 months. For all these ratios, higher is better and negative is bad. EY, SY and FY are medians of the inverse of Price/Earnings, Price/Sales and Price/Free Cash Flow. They are better for statistical studies than price-to-something ratios, which are unusable when the "something" is close to zero or negative (for example, companies with negative earnings). I also calculate two momentum metrics for each group: the median monthly return (RetM) and the median annual return (RetY).

I prefer medians rather than averages because a median splits a set in a good half and a bad half. Capital-weighted averages are skewed by extreme values and the largest companies. As a consequence, these metrics are designed for stock-picking rather than index investing.

Value and Quality Scores

Historical baselines are calculated as the averages on a look-back period of 11 years for all metrics. They are noted respectively EYh, SYh, FYh, ROEh, GMh. For example, the value of EYh for technology in the table below is the 11-year average of the median Earnings Yield of S&P 500 tech companies.

The Value Score "VS" is the average difference in % between the three valuation ratios (EY, SY, FY) and their baselines (EYh, SYh, FYh). The same way, the Quality Score "QS" is the average difference between the two quality ratios (ROE, GM) and their baselines (ROEh, GMh).

VS may be interpreted as the percentage of undervaluation or overvaluation relative to the baseline (positive is good, negative is bad). This interpretation must be taken with caution: the baseline is an arbitrary reference, not a supposed fair value. The formula assumes that the three valuation metrics are of equal importance, except in energy and utilities where the Free Cash Flow Yield is ignored to avoid some inconsistencies. VS and QS are capped between -100 and +100 when the calculation goes beyond these values.

Energy has been showing the best value and quality scores among GICS sectors since February 2022. Healthcare and communication services are undervalued by about 16% and 11% relative to 11-year averages, respectively. Consumer staples, real estate, consumer discretionary and financials are close to their historical baseline in valuation. Utilities are overvalued by 14% based on the same metrics. Industrials, technology and materials are overvalued by over 20%.

The S&P 500 (NYSEARCA:IVV) has gained 13.3% in 12 months, while the median return is 11% (reported in the table above) and the equal-weight average is at 8.2% (measured on RSP). It means the index performance has been moderately skewed to the upside by mega-cap companies over the last 12 months. The S&P mid-cap 400 index and the small cap index Russell 2000 have been lagging the large cap index (1.8% for MDY and 0.8% for IWM).

We use the table above to calculate value and quality scores. It may also be used in a stock-picking process to check how companies stand among their peers. For example, the EY column tells that a large consumer staples company with an Earnings Yield above 0.0468 (or price/earnings below 21.37) is in the better half of the sector regarding this metric. A Dashboard List is sent every month to Quantitative Risk & Value subscribers with the most profitable companies standing in the better half among their peers regarding the three valuation metrics at the same time. data.title: Armada Hoffler: The Knife Has Fallen, Time To Pick It Up
data.description:
My previous article on Armada Hoffler Properties, Inc. (AHH) was issued on February 2025 before the dividend cut was announced. The title of the article was: Armada Hoffler Properties: Catching A Falling Knife Or Diamond? I'm Still Long. As the title indicates, I was rather bullish on AHH. The main driver for my bullishness was the assumption of lower base rates and the associated tailwinds that should help AHH gradually emerge out of a very tight dividend coverage level. Plus, the underlying business seemed rather robust.

However, since the publication of my piece, the stock is down by ~20%.

The dividend cut in combination with additional equity raise and weaker than expected AFFO guidance gave the market a strong reason to assign even more aggressive risk premiums to AHH's valuation. Plus, the volatility in the long-term yields and the tariff-induced uncertainty have not helped AHH either.

Now, I think that by looking at the title of the current article, it should be clear what my stance is on AHH. I know that the ~20% decline after my previous thesis is not encouraging and does not necessarily provide a confidence that this time the total return will be acceptable.

I fully get it.

Having said that, let me still articulate my thinking on why, in my view, AHH presents a very compelling risk to reward play.

Thesis Review

Let's start with the important basics.

Because of the massive share price decline, but actually stable FFO generation, the P/FFO multiple has dropped to 6.7x, which is almost perfectly in line with the hotel REIT sector median multiple. The thing is that hotel REITs tend to be the lowest priced players in the entire REIT universe because their cash flows are inherently volatile (i.e., there are no long-term lease agreements) and extremely sensitive to the business cycles. In fact, currently, we are in a period of uncertainty, where the sentiment is negative. This means that the hotel REIT multiple is lower than average. And even considering these nuances, there is no difference between AHH's multiple and that of relatively depressed hotel REITs.

Furthermore, the forward dividend yield for AHH stands at 7.9%. This is quite high despite the recent dividend cut by ~32%, which has normalized the AFFO payout to ~70%.

Again, the reason for this attractive yield lies in the combination of dirt cheap multiple and strong cash flow generation.

The question is whether the AFFO generation per share is stable. If we can conclude that the AFFO is actually stable, then this fact alone should make the case attractive enough for a buy (e.g., rich dividend with a notable multiple recovery potential).

Here are my three key reasons why I think that the AFFO per share could be deemed sustainable.

First, the underlying business remains in a solid shape. For example, in Q1 2025 figures we can see that the overall portfolio had an occupancy ratio of 95% across all three segments (office, retail and high-multiple multifamily). For many, the office segment is what causes a concern. Yet, exactly this segment had the best occupancy level at 97.5%, and there are no meaningful lease expirations through 2027.

Apart from the occupancy aspect, the total portfolio NOI increased by 2% on a year-over-year basis, which is certainly not indicative of a declining asset base. Plus, and this is important to appreciate even though it might seem as a small detail, the Q1 2025 AHH's operational cost base has decreased by 13% compared to the same period last year.

As a result of stable occupancy, steadily growing NOI and improved cost base, the AFFO per share in Q1 landed at $0.20, which is by $0.02 higher than in the prior quarter.

Second, the capital structure remains aggressive. While the stabilized debt to EBITDA is 5.4x (i.e., a conservative statistic), the total or consolidated metric is 7.1x, which is quite high. However, I do not think that this should be a reason to avoid AHH, especially considering the following mitigants:

  • The AFFO payout is 70%, which means that 30% of the cash generation is kept at the REIT's books and could be used for debt reduction (i.e., we can already see a progress here).
  • The fact that the AFFO per share is advancing higher translates to a larger cash flow retention base that could also be used for debt reduction activity. I really doubt that AHH will increase the dividend in the foreseeable future, which is totally fine given where the current yield is.
  • The consensus indicates that the base rates are set to go down in the near to medium-term, which means that there will be some positive boost to the AFFO generation through a reduced interest cost burden (although I would not put a huge expectation/bet here).

Speaking of AFFO stability, another nuance that we have to factor in is AHH's lease profile. Namely, this year and in 2026, AHH has only 3% and 4%, respectively, of total leases expiring. This provides another tailwind for slowly but surely reinvesting the retained AFFO into the balance sheet optimization program.

Third, the consensus market expectation indicates the following FFO per share trajectory:

2025 - FFO per share of $1.05.

2026 - FFO per share of $1.06.

2027 - FFO per share of $1.16.

Again, it seems that the analysts agree with me that the underlying business is growing. What these figures also imply is that the cash flow retention levels will gradually go up, which, as articulated above, should offer a notable support for the management in working down the total debt load that is currently on the aggressive side.

Finally, if we now turn back to the valuation aspect. I think that by digesting the aforementioned data and facts, we can safely conclude that AHH is not falling off the cliff. The business is stable (e.g., occupancy high, NOI growing, favorable lease expiry profile) and the dividend is well-covered.

Now, let's also consider this:

  • AHH's P/FFO multiple is 6.7x.
  • The REIT segment category (diversified) in which AHH is placed has an average multiple of 10.0x.
  • 13.5% of AHH's leases stem from multifamily, which has an average multiple of 20.9x.
  • 25% of AHH's leases are placed in retail segment, which has an average multiple of 13.7x.
  • Then the rest goes to the category that is called "mixed use", which consists of a combination of multifamily, retail and office. And the office REIT segment has an average multiple of 9.4x.

Everywhere we look, AHH is severely discounted. While I do not have an exact estimate for what the justified multiple could exactly be, but my base case is that if AHH manages to prove the market that the AFFO per share is sustainable and even slightly growing, then the multiple should revert back to the low double-digit level (or at minimum to the office-level multiple).

Key Risks

There are obviously some risks involved in the case here. The market is not that irrational. Namely, there is a reason why AHH is heavily discounted (although I do not agree with the level of discount).

While I do not see any material risks on the property side due to the resilient operational performance (e.g., healthy occupancy, like-for-like NOI growth), I am a bit worried about the leverage.

As articulated above, a debt to EBITDA of 7.1x is high. So, there is a very tiny margin of error embedded in the books. And the real risk here lies in the refinancing dynamic, where if the long-term yields rise, AHH will have to inevitably experience notable headwinds to the AFFO generation, which potentially might even lead to an AFFO contraction.

However, there are two reasonable mitigants in place:

  • The debt maturity profile is laddered, meaning that the bulk of maturities is back-end loaded (after 2026).
  • The current weighted average interest rate is 4.2%, which is not that far off from the AHH on-the-run term loan facility rates (~6.3%).

The Bottom-Line

AHH is a high risk REIT, no question about that.

Yet, we have to put all of this into the context of dirt-cheap multiple, well-covered and high dividend and a growing core business.

In my view, the risk and reward profile is attractive, providing a decent basis for investors to go long.

In a nutshell, I remain bullish on AHH.

data.title: Alphabet Stock Is A Value Trap
data.description:
Right now, I am seeing increasingly many value investors pitch Alphabet Inc. aka Google (NASDAQ:GOOG) (NASDAQ:GOOGL) (TSX:GOOG:CA) as a great investment opportunity. Nearly everyone has a "Strong Buy" rating for it on Seeking Alpha.

Its share price has recently sold off, even as the company kept growing its cash flow. As a result, it now trades at a historically low valuation:

Current P/E 10-year Average P/E
Alphabet 19x 26x

Here, you can also see its historic P/E multiple in a chart. You can clearly see that it is historically low:

Despite being so cheap, as I write this, analysts are still projecting the company to grow its earnings per share by about 12% through 2029.

So what's not to like?

Low valuation, coupled with rapid growth, is a recipe for high returns. Add some multiple expansion to the growth, and you could double your money within the next 5 years.

Even then, I am not buying it.

On the contrary, this is one of the last tech stocks that I would dare to buy in today's rapidly evolving world, and it all comes down to my personal experience as a user of Google.

I have replaced 90%+ of my Google searches with AIs like ChatGPT, and I don't see myself going back.

Why This Is Very Negative for Google

First off, you need to recognize that search is still Google's golden goose.

Yes, it has greatly diversified its business over the past decades with YouTube, Google Play, Android, Chrome, Cloud, Waymo, and other smaller bets.

But Search still makes up about 50% of Alphabet's revenue, and since this is a high-margin business, it makes up closer to 2/3 of its profits.

Therefore, if users like me shift away from Google search, its main profit engine will be at risk.

And this is happening right now.

AI tools like ChatGPT, Claude, Perplexity, and Grok are far more efficient at finding the right information than Google.

Instead of having to click on 10 links, you get answers right away.

They are replacing the process of "searching" with direct answers. It is far faster and more efficient in most cases.

I think that I am just a bit ahead of the crowd, but I expect most people to move in this direction and gradually replace "searching" with AI assistants.

Here, you might say that Alphabet is also developing its own AI tools, and you are right. Google is one of the leaders in AI.

But here's the issue:

AI Will Cannibalize Their Search Business

The Google search business is so insanely profitable because of the whole process of "searching."

You are clicking 10 links to find the right information, and you come across countless ads in the process, printing cash for Google.

But if Google goes all in on AI, it will cannibalize its most profitable business.

People will search far less since they are getting direct answers with AI assistants. This results in far fewer impressions and less ad inventory.

Therefore, even if Google is successful at building AI tools, it still won't be able to monetize them as efficiently as its Search business.

There is also no guarantee that they will even succeed. Their AI tools are promising, but monetizing them may also prove challenging given that so many of them are completely free.

Bottom Line: Google is in a Tough Spot

They have two options:

They can stick with search and hope that AI assistants won't get adopted to the same degree as I am already using them.

Or they can go all in on AI, but this will hurt their most profitable business.

In a way, they find themselves in a similar situation to Kodak when digital photography took off, and they realized that it would kill their film photography business.

There is no good solution.

Both options will likely lead to much lower profits over time. I strongly believe that Google search will fall behind in AI adoption because I have already changed my habits, and it is far better this way.

It will force them to invest most heavily in AI, but they will struggle with monetization because you don't click on 5 ads when you are in an AI chatbot. If they overwhelm you with ads, you will simply use another AI tool like ChatGPT.

So, how I see it is that Google has lost its monopoly. AI tools are replacing search, and this is a real existential crisis for the company.

With that in mind, I have no interest in buying shares of the company.

I am not a short seller, but if I were looking for a tech company to short, Google is one I would consider. The valuation multiple is likely still too high, and so are the analyst estimates for future growth.

data.title: Kuaishou Technology: High-Teens EPS Growth Is Plausible
data.description:
Investment summary

My previous investment thought on Kuaishou Technology (OTCPK:KUASF) was a buy rating because the valuation was still cheap and I expected growth to accelerate. I reiterate my buy rating as the earnings growth outlook is still solid, supported by reacceleration in advertising, continued momentum in e-commerce, and a strong comeback in live-streaming. Engagement held up well, and Kling AI is also seeing real monetization traction.

1Q25 results update

KUASF delivered 1Q25 RMB32.6 billion in total revenue, up 10.9% y/y. By segments, live-streaming was up 14% y/y, online marketing services were up 8% y/y, and the other services segment (mainly e-commerce) grew 15.2% y/y. Supporting the e-commerce revenue growth was GMV growth of 15.4%, driving it to RMB332.3 billion. The business continues to show solid resilience when it comes to user growth and engagement. In 1Q25, average DAUs hit a record 408 million, up 3.6% y/y, while MAUs grew 2.1% y/y to 712 million. More importantly, engagement remains strong, and this is perhaps the most important metric to track. In 1Q25, average daily time spent per user was 133.8 minutes, and this drove a 5.9% y/y increase in total user time spent on the platform.

Gross margin came in at 54.6%, translating to RMB17.8 billion in gross profit. Adj. net income was RMB4.6 billion, but margin fell by 90bps to 14%, mainly due to negative movements in the cost structure. Operating expenses were up—sales and marketing by 5.5%, R&D by 16%, and admin costs jumped 79.2%.

Advertising and Ecommerce Positive Outlook, Live Streaming Recovery

Some investors may be worried about the advertising segment growth slowdown in 1Q25—the segment grew only by 8% vs. 13% in 4Q24 and 27% in 1Q24—but I think this segment is poised for a re-acceleration. There are a few key drivers supporting my view. Firstly, KUASF will start to face easier y/y comps as it moves through the remaining quarters. 1Q24 was the toughest comp in FY24, with 27% y/y growth. Secondly, underlying demand is evidently still very strong in content-linked ad verticals. For instance, short plays and mini-games showed robust performance in 1Q25, while ad spend from local services clients grew >50% y/y (source: 1Q25 earnings call). Lastly, there is a visible and structural catalyst to accelerate great monetization of shelf-based e-commerce traffic (i.e., the searching and browsing traffic). So far, this channel currently contributes little to ad revenue, but I think the scale potential is significant because management noted that this type of traffic accounts for a huge portion of total GMV (around 30% in 1Q25).

For e-commerce, as I noted in the 4Q24 update, momentum remains strong, with 1Q25 GMV up 15.4% y/y to RMB332.3 billion. What is more impressive this time round is the improvement in growth mix. Monthly active paying users hit 135 million (vs. 126 million in 1Q24); merchant engagement has gotten a lot better (new merchant onboarding grew >30% y/y); daily active merchants in the shopping mall ecosystem grew >40%; and short video e-commerce also held up well, with GMV growing >40% y/y. These are very positive signs that suggest the ecosystem is expanding and is getting more active.

Pan-shelf-based e-commerce also continues to contribute ~30% of total GMV and is growing faster than the overall segment. Just to reiterate this important point: this metric signals a positive shift toward organic discovery, and this has a significant long-term impact on margins because users are increasingly finding products via search and recommendations rather than relying on KOLs or short videos, which are costlier to scale.

Finally, the main highlight of this quarter, I believe, is KUASF's live-streaming business, which saw a strong comeback, with revenue up 14.4% y/y to RMB9.8 billion. Remember, this segment has been a drag on the business for the entire FY24, so this rebound reflects improvements in execution. I believe the improvement is structural and should stay, as management has clearly doubled down on content quality and operational focus. Firstly, they have increased the number of partner talent agencies by 25% y/y and grown the number of streamers under management by 40%. These efforts are clearly working, and I think it makes sense because KUASF now has more control over content delivered. Secondly, the “live-streaming+” strategy is gaining real traction in real-world sectors like jobs and housing, with Kwai Hire and Ideal Housing seeing resume submissions and lead generation increase by >100% and 150% y/y, respectively.

However, I would temper readers’ expectations for the rest of FY25 that this segment is unlikely to continue printing double-digit revenue growth since 1Q24 was an easy comp, and 2H24 comp will be a lot tougher. What I like to see is at least positive growth, and if this is true, it would give a huge validation to strategies implemented (more quality control and live-streaming+), and it sets up well for FY26 to see positive growth again.

Kling AI: Monetization and Tech Strength

In 1Q25, Kling generated >RMB150 million in revenue (remember my guess previously was just RMB300 million for the full year), which I consider a strong signal that KUASF is seeing real monetization traction. A notable aspect beneath the headline number is the revenue mix. It is already seeing contributions from professional and consumer subscriptions and also B2B API. This matters because subscriptions offer recurring, predictable revenue, while API-based monetization has the potential to scale quickly with enterprise adoption. This signals that Kling isn’t just a niche tool that users use for “fun”; it is really resonating with both individual creators willing to pay for advanced features and businesses integrating Kling into their own platforms and workflows. In other words, the market opportunity is significant.

Additionally, 70% of revenue came from overseas markets, which shows that Kling has global relevance and there is a clear path for scaling to a much larger magnitude. From an earnings contribution standpoint, I think we are near an inflection point where Kling starts to contribute positively as well. Management specifically noted Kling has hit a positive marginal profit at the inference level, which tells me that the unit economics are already heading in the right direction.

Given the momentum and that KUASF launched Kling AI 2.0 globally in April—Kling AI 2.0 is basically an enhanced version of 1.0. It helps to make the video look smoother, understand what users are looking for better, and just generally make everything look better. My sense is that this momentum will continue for the foreseeable future.

Valuation

For a business that is expected to compound EPS by high-teens percentage for the next three years, I still think 10x forward PE is too cheap, let alone the potential of Kling AI, which is gaining very solid momentum. As a relative comparison, Tencent and Meituan trade at 16x PE with low-teens EPS growth. I get that Tencent and Meituan may be businesses with a greater moat because of their business model vs. KUASF, but this discount seems too wide. The demand indicators and execution convince me that KUASF can meet consensus EPS growth expectations: low-to-mid-teens revenue growth + margin expansion from scale = high-teens EPS growth. If this materializes, I think 10x is simply too cheap, as it means the stock trades at just 8x FY28 PE. I believe an upwards re-rating will happen as the market recognizes KUASF’s ability to compound EPS.

Risk

The biggest risk here is advertising growth may not re-accelerate. As I said before, a weaker macro backdrop could weigh on advertiser budgets. Shelf-based e-commerce monetization also needs to be executed carefully, as an overly aggressive rollout could impact user experience. If growth doesn’t accelerate as expected, this could really hurt expectations since this is the largest operating segment.

Conclusion

My view for KUASF is a buy rating. The business continues to execute well, and I believe the consensus estimate for high-teens EPS growth is plausible. Given that the stock still trades at just 10x forward PE, I think the market has not appreciated the business's EPS growth ability. Once the market gains confidence in the margin trajectory and Kling’s ability to scale, I expect a meaningful re-rating to follow.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: I'm Buying CrowdStrike Before Earnings As The Market Is Underestimating The Growth Story (Upgrade)
data.description:
Performance Assessment

CrowdStrike (NASDAQ:CRWD) has beaten the market since my last 'Neutral/Hold' update on the stock:

Thesis

CrowdStrike is reporting its Q1 FY26 earnings (it has a Jan-ending fiscal year) on 3rd June after-market hours. Ahead of this release, I am changing my stance to become a cautious bull on CRWD stock:

  1. Multiple channel checks point to higher-than-expected demand conditions
  2. Workforce reductions can be a margin lever
  3. There is some valuation risk
  4. CRWD is breaking out to the upside on the technical charts
  5. Margin benefits being eaten up by stock-based compensation is a key risk to monitor

Multiple channel checks point to higher-than-expected demand conditions

Many sources suggest that demand for CrowdStrike's solutions is very healthy and higher than expectations. For example, investment firm BTIG did 7 channel checks on CrowdStrike and had 6 responses indicating either stronger than expected Q1 FY26 demand or upbeat directional commentary. It looks like CrowdStrike is seeing good activity among resellers of its products and solutions as well, with cross-selling success without compromising on pricing:

Overall we see the setup as somewhat positive following favorable reseller checks both at RSA and over the past several weeks. Specifically, we heard continued success with cross-selling efforts and no abnormal discounts as resellers remain positive on the company's FY/26 outlook

- RBC analyst Matthew Hedberg

RSA refers to the RSA Cybersecurity Conference

A lot of the revenue momentum is driven by a surge in AI-related cybersecurity intelligence (cyber-AI) spending. I expect this trend to accelerate as customers ramp up on adopting CrowdStrike's Charlotte agentic AI solutions to SOCs within organizations [security operations center]:

There's a profound difference between adding AI features and fundamentally transforming how cybersecurity works. Charlotte AI goes beyond augmenting humans with suggestions - it actively investigates, reasons, and responds autonomously within expert-defined guardrails. Our agentic AI innovation represents a fundamental shift from reactive to proactive security - furthering CrowdStrike's mission of stopping breaches.

- CrowdStrike's Founder and CEO George Kurtz

In my coverage of other companies such as Palantir (PLTR), NVIDIA (NVDA), and Microsoft (MSFT), I have talked more about why agentic AI applications are a big growth driver that still seems to be underappreciated and unaccounted for in analysts and investor projections of companies' financials. And it looks like this may be true for CrowdStrike as well, as the aforementioned growth drivers have not resulted in any wave of upward revenue revisions by Wall St. consensus:

Currently, the consensus opinion is penciling in $1.1 billion in Q1 FY26 revenues for CrowdStrike based on Capital IQ data. This represents a revenue growth deceleration to about 20% YoY:

I think this is understating it a bit. I would expect at least 25% YoY growth. Hence, I see scope for a ~4.3% beat on revenue results and some upside to future guidance, as well as management, providing affirmation of some of these channel check insights.

Workforce reductions can be a margin lever

The incorporation of AI productivity tools into CrowdStrike's organization is enabling the company to reduce headcount intensity. This has prompted CEO George Kurtz to initiate layoffs to cut down 5% of the workforce. The timing of these cuts seems opportune, as CrowdStrike has reduced headcount needs after ending its Customer Commitment Package [CCP] in Mar'25.

The Customer Commitment Package [CCP] was CrowdStrike's response to win back the trust of its customers after the July 2024 outage incident, where a change in their Falcon endpoint detection and response agent led to widespread system failures and disruptions globally. The CCP gives CrowdStrike's customers free access to some products, extended payment terms and contract durations, and training opportunities.

Based on Jan'25 headcount numbers, a 5% cut would shrink the workforce to around 9600 employees:

Now, assuming that these 5% headcount costs are captured in overall OPEX (which I define as SG&A and R&D costs) my estimates indicate an OPEX margin reduction of 380bps (76.2% to 72.4%):

If this benefit is not spent anywhere else, including in stock-based compensation [SBC] (discussed more in the risks and monitorable section below), this can help the company transition to GAAP-EBIT margin profitability:

There is some valuation risk

CRWD stock is not cheap. It is currently trading at a 136x 1-yr fwd PE, which is 59% higher than the longer-term median of 86x:

So naturally, there is some valuation risk here. However, note that the growth traction is extremely healthy, with the company expected to grow at close to 20% YoY for the next 2 years:

I am wary of making another big error of omission by avoiding a stock purely for valuation reasons, especially when the revenue and margin outlook are expected to improve meaningfully.

CRWD is breaking out to the upside on the technical charts

If this is your first time reading a Hunting Alpha article using Technical Analysis, you may want to read this post, which explains how and why I read the charts the way I do. All my charts reflect total shareholder returns as they are adjusted for dividends/distributions.

Relative Read of CRWD vs. SPX500

CRWD vs. SPX500 looks like it is breaking out to the upside in the monthly charts. May'25's monthly candle has tested the monthly resistance level and bounced higher from it, leading to a resistance-turned-support flip. Hence, I expect further upside and hence outperformance to follow.

Margin benefits being eaten up by stock-based compensation is a key risk to monitor

It is logical to assume that headcount cuts would also reduce stock-based compensation [SBC] as that is essentially a means of paying employees. If this is true, we can potentially see a drop in SBC to ~17% of revenues (assuming the OPEX margin drop is fully reflected in SBC):

But I have a worry that even this may not happen because there has been no decrease in SBC margin intensity despite revenue growth outpacing headcount growth for most of the past 10 quarters:

This has been one of my biggest issues with the stock; a lot of the value seems to be captured by employees instead of the minority shareholders.

Takeaway & Positioning

CrowdStrike is reporting its Q1 FY26 results on Tuesday in the upcoming week. I am positioning myself cautiously bullish beforehand. This is because multiple sources point to higher-than-expected demand conditions, which do not seem to be reflected in the consensus revenue estimates so far. So I think there is ample scope for both a revenue result and guidance beat. Also, I see a 380bps tailwind to margins driven by 5% headcount cuts across the organization. But a risk to watch out for here is these headcount cuts not flowing through to the GAAP-bottom line and instead being captured by stock-based compensation. Principally, this would benefit employees but not minority shareholders like us.

I also acknowledge that there is some valuation risk, but I am wary of placing too much weight on this and making an error of omission, ignoring the arguably more important aspect of strong and improving momentum in revenues and margins. Also, CRWD vs. SPX500 technicals give some confidence for the buys as the stock is breaking out of a monthly resistance level.

Rating: 'Buy'

How to interpret Hunting Alpha's ratings:

Strong Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher-than-usual confidence. I also have a net long position in the security in my personal portfolio.

Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis.

Neutral/hold: Expect the company to perform in line with the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis.

Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis.

Strong Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence.

The typical time-horizon for my view is multiple quarters to more than a year. It is not set in stone. However, I will share updates on my changes in stance in a pinned comment to this article and may also publish a new article discussing the reasons for the change in view.

data.title: Levi Strauss: Holding Up Its Pants
data.description:
Levi Strauss (NYSE:LEVI) recently hit the newswires after the company sold its iconic Dockers brand. My last view on the business goes back to the spring of 2020 when the world was grappling with the outbreak of the pandemic.

Operating with strong brands, a strong balance sheet and diversified operations, the company was entering a tough time in a solid shape after going public in the summer of 2019, making it a decent play in my view.

Shares ended up rallying to the $30 mark in 2021 and ever since have largely traded in a $15-$25 range, with shares now trading at the lower end of the range. While Levi is a strong brand and a solid investment, I fail to be upbeat despite a cheap earnings multiple.

A Jeans Wear Giant

Levi Strauss & Co. is one of the world's largest brand-name apparel businesses, best known for its namesake brand, which makes up the vast majority of sales. This is complemented by its Dockers brand and Denizen and Signature brands.

The company generates over $6 billion in sales with products found in some 50,000 retail locations, half of which are in the Americas, complemented by larger operations in Europe and to a lesser extent in Asia.

Two-thirds of sales are derived from men, with 75% of sales tied to bottoms, complemented by tops, footwear, and accessories, as the company has been looking to grow the non-denim, non-men business.

Modest Progress

A $5.8 billion business in 2019 has made gradual inroads as revenues have risen in a modest fashion to $6.36 billion in 2024. GAAP net earnings were reported at $210 million, equal to $0.52 per share, substantially lower than adjusted earnings which were reported at $1.25 per share, with the difference attributed to restructuring charges and goodwill impairment charges.

The company ended 2024 with a net debt load of $304 million as the 400 million shares started the year around the $17 mark, granting equity a $6.8 billion equity valuation, and the entire firm a $7.1 billion valuation. This values the business at around 1.1 times sales and 8.4 times EBITDA of $842 million. With earnings power reported at $1.25 per share, multiples come in at 13-14 times adjusted earnings. These are very reasonable multiples, but this comes amidst modest growth of the business in recent years, and quite some charges taken to these earnings.

The company guided for flattish performance in the fiscal year 2025, with reported sales seen down 1-2% and adjusted EBITDA seen flattish between $1.20 and $1.25 per share.

For the first quarter, which ended in March of the year, the company reported a 3% increase in sales to $1.53 billion, with organic revenue growth reported at 9%, which is adjusted for an anticipated divestment of the Dockers brand. The quarter was very strong, with adjusted earnings up thirteen cents to $0.38 per share. Following the strong start to the year, the company maintained the outlook for the year, despite the uncertainty induced by tariffs.

Streamlining Operations

Soon after the release of the first quarter results, Levi Strauss announced that it had reached a deal to divest Dockers to the Authentic Brands Group. Deal terms call for a $311 million transaction value, with earn outs potentially increasing this number to $391 million, based on the performance of the activities down the road.

With the sale, Levi's aims to focus more on its namesake brand and other strategic goals such as women, denim, and yoga. Based on the 2024 results, Dockers generated $323 million in annual sales, down 4% from the year before. This suggests that the headline price tag comes in around 1.0 times sales, and about 1.2 times if the earn-out is included. This is largely in line with the overall valuation, as the margins of this business were not separately announced.

Frankly, this is a bolt-on divestment, set to reduce net debt of $412 million in the first quarter to about $100 million here, excluding the earn-out. This means that modest net debt will evaporate, but Levis will see about 5% of sales walk out of the door.

With earnings power seen stable around $1.25 per share, and this deal likely doing little to change that, the overall 13-14 times earnings multiple remains intact, driven by the resilient operations of the business, as frankly, shares have been doing alright during the peak of tariffs and uncertainty.

What Now?

After shares hit a low of $12 amidst peak uncertainty around tariffs in recent weeks, shares have rebounded to $17 here, trading at par with levels at the start of the year.

A net unleveraged balance sheet and a reasonable 13-14 times earnings multiple and 3% dividend yield look compelling, but frankly I see limited room for multiple expansions in the apparel businesses, as these have remained tough long-term investment propositions.

A re-rating is tough to get here, even amidst a very strong brand and balance sheet. While I expect shares to do reasonably well, I fail to see a very compelling risk-reward here, despite a fair divestment of Dockers, after shares have recently seen quite a move higher.

data.title: Canadian Natural Resources: My Number 1 Energy Pick
data.description:
Article Thesis

Canadian Natural Resources (NYSE:CNQ)(TSX:CNQ:CA) is an energy company that combines many positives: Strong growth, a nice dividend yield, resilience versus low oil prices and macro shocks, and so on. Add an undemanding valuation, and CNQ looks very compelling — it's not risk-free but looks like a good investment to me.

Past Articles

I have written about Canadian Natural Resources here on Seeking Alpha in the past, most recently in early March, around three months ago. I gave the company a "Strong Buy" rating back then, which has worked out well so far, as CNQ has delivered a total return of 10% since then, easily beating the broad market's return of 3% over the same time frame. However, I believe that this is only the beginning of the returns that we can expect from CNQ in the coming years. With one quarter having passed since my last article, and with CNQ reporting its most recent earnings results since my last article on the company, it is time to update my views today.

CNQ And The Macro Picture

Canadian Natural Resources has, compared to many other energy companies, a relatively resilient business model thanks to low break-even costs that allow it to remain profitable in most energy price environments. Still, the company is impacted by energy price movements, of course, as higher energy prices mean higher profits while lower energy prices mean lower profits, all else equal.

The macro picture is thus important for CNQ, like its peers. In 2025, oil prices have come down so far, with WTI crude oil (CL1:COM) trading in the low-$60s right now. This is largely due to worries about global demand, with some analysts and investors seeing the current trade tensions between the US and other markets (Europe, China, ...) as a reason for lower oil demand in the future. While it is possible that oil demand will take a hit during a potential tariff-induced recession, players like OPEC are forecasting solid demand growth for the current year as well as for next year on a global basis, at 1.3 million barrels per day for each year. This is driven by healthy economic growth in markets such as India, by growing air traffic, and so on — even if oil demand were to decline somewhat in the US, demand growth in other markets could more than offset that headwind. I thus am not bearish on oil markets in general, as the underlying demand growth trend, driven by fast-growth markets such as China and India, remains intact. Geopolitical crises and shocks could result in supply disruptions, e.g. if tensions with Iran were to escalate — it is thus possible that oil prices rise substantially from here, while I think that it is unlikely that they will fall a lot from here.

Compared to the trading range over the last couple of years, oil is currently rather inexpensive: The three-year trading range is around $55 to $115, oil thus seems relatively cheap right now, with price normalization likely being more of a tailwind than a headwind going forward.

CNQ: Very Compelling Operational Performance

While oil price movements and, to a lesser degree, natural gas price movements impact CNQ's results, so does the way the company operates. Canadian Natural Resources' ability to keep cost controls tight and to grow production impacts profitability and cash flow generation a lot as well, and things looked pretty good here in the recent past. Earlier in May, the company showcased its most recent earnings results, delivering what, I think, was a very compelling operational result.

Canadian Natural Resources was able to grow its output, measured in barrels of oil equivalent per day, to 1.58 million during the most recent quarter, which was up by a very strong 19% compared to an output of 1.33 Mboed one year earlier. Usually, major oil companies grow their production by a couple of percentage points per year, if they grow their production at all, but CNQ grew its output by almost 20% in a single year. This was partially due to M&A, mainly the takeover of the AOSP and Duverney assets in the fourth quarter. But even adjusted for that, production growth would be significantly ahead of the peer group — in-situ production, for example, was up 6% year-over-year as well and was not impacted by M&A.

At the same time, Canadian Natural Resources' cost performance was strong, with light crude oil production costs dropping 14% on a per-barrel basis while North American natural gas production costs declined by 9%. Even better, CNQ's thermal in-situ production costs dropped by a hefty 20% on a per-barrel basis during the most recent quarter. This is the result of efficiency investments, operating leverage, and cost-cutting measures such as automation. Management seems to be very focused on keeping cost controls tight and improving efficiencies where it is possible to do so, which I like very much as a shareholder. After all, production growth alone wouldn't be overly attractive if it came at the cost of declining efficiency, but that is clearly not the case at CNQ.

Between these factors, CNQ managed to generate adjusted funds from operations of C$4.5 billion during the quarter, or C$18 billion annualized. Even after accounting for capital expenditures, both for maintenance and future growth, planned at C$6 billion this year, that would mean C$12 billion of free funds flow — available for dividends, buybacks, and debt reduction. Since Canadian Natural Resources already has a relatively clean balance sheet, there is no need to focus on debt reduction too much, which is why the majority of the company's free funds flow/free cash flow will go towards shareholder returns, which brings us to another major pro argument for an investment in CNQ.

CNQ's Shareholder Returns

Due to the fact that energy price movements can result in swings in the profits of energy companies, they oftentimes don't have long dividend growth track records. CNQ is a bit of an outlier, however — it has raised its dividend for 25 years in a row, giving it Dividend Aristocrat status. But unlike Chevron (CVX) and ExxonMobil (XOM), which have also decades-long dividend growth streaks, CNQ is growing its dividend very quickly:

Over the last five years, CNQ grew its dividend by around 170% — around 5x the growth seen at CVX, and 15x the growth seen at XOM. At the same time, CNQ also had the best pace of share repurchases, reducing its share count by double digits on a net basis. Some other energy companies have achieved high dividend growth rates in recent years as well, but those have oftentimes cut their dividend during the pandemic, meaning they started from a low starting point. CNQ combines a long dividend growth streak, spanning all kinds of crises, with a very strong dividend growth rate, both over the last 25 years and over the last couple of years. Note: The bump in the CNQ dividend chart is due to a special dividend in 2022.

To make things even better, CNQ offers a strong yield of 5.2% right now, which is well above the average for energy companies. A high starting yield, a fast dividend growth rate, a nice buyback pace, and a dividend that is safe during all kinds of crises result in a very attractive combination.

Final Thoughts

CNQ's oil sands assets are very low-decline, long-lasting assets. There is no meaningful exploration risk, and thanks to low break-even costs and declining production costs, CNQ is well-positioned to remain profitable in almost all macro environments. Ongoing growth investments and occasional takeovers result in a nice growth rate, and on a per-share basis, growth is even better, thanks to CNQ's buybacks.

If a recession were to happen, oil prices could decline from here, and CNQ would possibly see its shares pull back as well. But oil prices are currently closer to the lows of the recent trading range, which is why I do not believe that we will see another steep drop, and CNQ is already priced very inexpensively, at just ~5x Q's annualized funds from operations.

All in all, CNQ combines a strong return outlook, due to valuation upside, underlying business growth, a 5%+ yield, and buybacks, with considerable resilience, due to its defensive, low-risk assets and proven ability to grow the dividend during times of crisis — and that's why CNQ is currently my biggest energy investment.

data.title: Potentially Earn 12%-15% Income: Monthly Options Series (June 2025)
data.description:
Author's Note: This article is part of our periodic/monthly series that attempts to present two lists of stocks for the month that could be suitable for writing options to generate relatively safe income. Certain parts of the introduction, definitions, and section describing the selection process will have some commonality and repetitiveness with our previous articles in this series. This is unavoidable (as well as intentional) in keeping the entire series consistent and easy to follow for new readers. Such sections are presented in "italics." Regular readers who follow the series from month to month could skip such sections. Also, for more details on these sections, please refer to our blog post.

Why This Monthly Options Series?

Writing (or selling) options is not highly complicated, even though it does involve some learning curve and practice. Obviously, they're not for everyone. But if you are someone who is looking to potentially generate 10% to 15% income on a relatively consistent basis and is ready to put in the effort on a regular basis, we think you will find our monthly options series helpful. Option premiums are generally higher during periods of high volatility and vice versa. Also, premiums are higher for stocks that are more volatile compared to stocks that move in a tight range. They can also be impacted by earnings reports and/or the ex-dividend date falling during the option period.

Earning a decent income from your investments, significantly higher than the inflation rate, is always challenging. This has been especially true in the past decade and a half. In the last few years, interest rates have been elevated, but in most cases, the fixed deposit rates barely match the inflation rate. Furthermore, interest rates are now going to decline going forward, albeit at a slow pace. We believe selling options (cash-covered puts and covered calls) remain a relatively good choice for earning a high current income while conserving your investment capital. Obviously, there's a learning curve, and there are some risks involved with options, and we do not recommend blindly jumping into the game. We will discuss how to mitigate the risks in a bit. Also, we will urge due diligence.

We also offer an Options-Income portfolio to our HIDIY subscribers where we use roughly $75,000 to $100,000 of real-life money to write options on a regular basis.

All tables in this article have been created by the author (unless explicitly specified). Most of the data in this article is sourced from Fidelity, Yahoo Finance, DripInvesting, and Barchart.com.

Options Income Strategy 101

Note: This section is for readers who have not had much prior exposure to or experience with Options. We also describe the difference between "selling options" vs. "buying options."

How To Mitigate the Risks

We do not intend to convey an impression, especially to the folks who are new to options, that there's no risk in selling options. In fact, there's plenty, especially if we're not careful. However, there are ways we can minimize the risk by following certain time-tested principles. We encourage you to read our blog post, "How to mitigate risks with writing Options."

In brief, we cover the following in the above blog post:

Never use margin (or borrowed) money to sell/write options. Write options on stocks that you do not mind holding mid to long term. Preferably, use dividend-paying underlying stocks so that if a call is assigned, you get the dividend while waiting for them to recover. Do not write Call options on stocks or positions that you do not want to lose (called away) under any circumstances. Do not chase very high premiums; 10-15% premiums (annualized) should be good enough.

Selection Strategy For Underlying Stocks

Note: This section describes the broader selection process and is repeated every month for the benefit of new readers. Regular readers could skip this and jump to the next section.

The market cap of the company is near or higher than $10 billion (this can be lowered somewhat in a down market). Daily volume for the underlying stock to be > 10,000. Dividend yield preferably > 1.5%; some exceptions are made.

By applying the above criteria, we get roughly 600 stocks.

We will calculate a "dividend safety score" based on factors like dividend history, past dividend growth, debt/capital, return on capital, credit rating, and EPS growth. Sure, a high safety score would not guarantee absolute safety because business conditions can change over time, new competition can emerge, or the management can get distracted or make some bad decisions, destroying shareholder value. Nonetheless, a high dividend safety score will at least provide a reasonable level of assurance that the company has the financial capability to continue making its dividend payments for the foreseeable future.

We're going to use our proprietary formulas (as detailed below) to calculate the optimal strike prices for call and put options. However, there are many other ways to determine the appropriate strike prices. You could use the Greek metrics, like delta, gamma, theta, etc., from your brokers' site to help select optimal strike prices. Readers are encouraged to try several methods before determining what works best for them. There are many other ways to determine the appropriate strike prices.

We will calculate the following ratios and factors:

The Distance ratio:

Distance-Ratio = (52-WK-HIGH - 52-WK-LOW)/((52-WK-HIGH + 52-WK-LOW)/2)

Distance-Ratio % = Distance-Ratio x 100

Strike-Price Safe Distance

Strike-Price-Safe-Distance % = [(Distance-Ratio %) x STPR-factor (STRIKE-PRICE-factor)] / 10

Whereas STPRC-factor = 1.2 (can vary from 1.0 to 1.5)

Note: The STPRC-Factor can be adjusted based on how volatile the underlying stock is. If the stock is highly volatile, the factor should be adjusted to a higher value like 1.5, whereas it can be set to a lower band like 1.2 (or less) for low-volatility stocks.

CALL Option Strike-price = Close-price + (Close-price x Strike-Price-Safe-Distance)

This price may need to be rounded to the lowest dollar or half-dollar amount depending upon what strike prices are prevailing for the underlying stock for the specific strike date.

PUT Option Strike-price = Close-price - (Close-price x Strike-Price-Safe-Distance)

This price may need to be rounded up to the nearest dollar or half-dollar amount, depending upon what strike prices are prevailing for the underlying stock for the specific strike date.

There are many ways to determine the appropriate strike prices. However, we have chosen to use our above proprietary formulas to calculate the optimal strike prices for the sake of ease and simplicity.

Option Candidates For The Next Month

Below, we present two lists of 10 stocks each, one for writing put options and the other for writing call options. For call options, we buy the underlying stocks and sell deep-in-the-money calls at the same time. Our goal is simply to earn a high income (and not own these stocks), as, more than likely, those underlying shares would be called away. Please note that some stocks may appear in both lists as they may satisfy the criteria for more than one category.

10 Option Stocks Suitable For Put Options

The market is near its all-time high. Also, the candidates for the put options are biased toward momentum stocks, which means these stocks may be trading very close to their 52-week highs. If markets were to crash for any reason during the option period, we would likely get assigned these stocks. So, please use caution.

For put options, our primary objective is to generate income. We do not wish to hold these stocks as much as possible, so we would want to see them expire worthless. To identify such stocks, we select stocks that have had a rising trend recently. These stocks generally would have high relative strength and positive momentum. We will analyze the one-week, four-week, and 12-week price performance as well as relative strength data and try to see if the underlying stock is having a rising trend or a downward trend.

Momentum Score = (1Week-Perf)*2 + (4Week-Perf)*1 + (12Week-Perf)/2

The above formula gives higher weightage to more recent momentum.

We sort the list based on the momentum score and cross-check with the relative strength and composite rating (sourced from IBD - Investor Business Daily, subscription required). Also, we try to avoid stocks with very high market prices (above $250 per share) as the cost per option contract becomes prohibitive, especially for folks with limited capital. However, some exceptions are made at times. We finally selected 10 stocks for put options.

If the outcome of the option is not favorable at expiry, and the option does get assigned (we will be put the shares in such cases), the rising trend will help write a fresh call option immediately, possibly with a good premium.

In our list of 10 candidates, we should be careful not to put too many names from the same industry segment. We generally limit to two names from the same sector to avoid too much concentration in one sector.

A word of caution on put options: Do not start a put option on a stock that you do not see yourself holding for an extended period of time. Also, please note that this list only highlights probable good candidates, but further due diligence may be required.

Here are the top 10 large-cap stocks for PUT options:

(NRG), (HWM), (PM), (FNV), (AVGO), (RL), (MOS), (APH), (GILD), and (BK).

Please note that we have generally excluded stocks that have a share price greater than $250 a share. At times, one or two exceptions are made.

Table 1:

Below, we present the current put options trades and likely current premiums that we can expect for the above 10 stocks.

Table 1A:

Please note that the premiums for put options are a bit subdued. We have taken the option prices during the weekend when the market was closed, so the bid/ask spreads were a bit wider. The option prices can change quickly based on the market sentiments and direction of the underlying stock. Our average discount (distance from the current price to the strike price) is relatively good, at -11.4% (on average).

Caution: Please note that this month, we have only one stock with its earnings report due during the option period. Any surprises in the earnings report can cause volatility in the stock price. Earnings releases can cause surprises on the positive or negative side, adding extra risk to the trade. Please use due diligence.

10 Option Stocks With Relatively Safe Dividends:

In this category, our selection criteria are different from those for put options in the first category. In this set, the emphasis is on the quality of the stock rather than high recent momentum. We will use our dataset, especially the dividend-related data, to calculate a score called the "Dividend Safety and Quality Score." However, there's nothing that we can claim to be absolutely safe in the investing world – so it's only relative safety. We use various financial metrics, such as payout ratios, debt-to-capital ratios, S&P credit rating, top-line growth, and dividend growth over the years. Based on the above factors and EPS rating, we calculate the dividend safety score.

In this category, we will use "deep-in-the-money" buy-write call options. It basically involves two (simultaneous) legs to the option contract. First, we buy the underlying stock (100 shares for one contract) at the current market price, and in the second leg, we sell a call option at a strike price that's nearly 7%-12% below the current market price. During times of high market volatility, we may even go for even higher discounts. In fact, both legs are traded at the same time, and your cost basis is the market price minus the premium you received. This cost basis is what you use in your limit orders. Your final cost basis may further be reduced by any dividend that you may earn/receive during the option period. If you already happen to own the stock from before, you can only use the second leg. Since our cost basis is usually 7-12% below the current market prices, this method offers an additional margin of safety.

We present ten such companies with high dividend safety scores. With these stocks, besides an average of 1-2% dividend, you can safely generate 10-15% additional income by writing covered-call options. The key is not to be greedy with premiums.

Our top 10 stocks with relatively safe dividends for (buy-write) call options are:

(BMI), (IMO), (AMAT), (V), (CTAS), (JPM), (MSFT), (GL), (APH), and (RGLD).

Please note that we have excluded most stocks that have a share price greater than $250 a share. However, we have made a couple of exceptions this time.

Table-2:

Note: 1) The "Dividend Rating" (shown as MOD FFI Quality Rating, the last column) is based on recent past parameters like dividend growth (one-year, three-year, and five-year), number of years of dividend growth, payout ratio based on cash flow (or EPS), ROC (return on capital), sales growth, debt/capital, credit rating, and EPS rating.

2). It's very important to know how much capital you're willing to commit and if you can really afford to allocate it. This strategy should only be used with stocks that you do not mind owning and holding for an extended period of time (short to mid term).

(BMI), (IMO), (AMAT), (V), (CTAS), (JPM), (MSFT), (GL), (APH), and (RGLD).

Table 3:

Caution: Please note that this month, the premiums are vastly lower compared to last month. Also, the earnings season is practically over, so we do not have any of the stocks with earnings reports due during our option period of roughly one month.

Note: 1. Some of the option prices have a wide bid/ask spread. In such cases, we have taken the mid-range prices based on bid/ask, and sometimes, lower than mid-range prices, but there's no guarantee that those prices would fill at all times. It's always recommended to use limit orders only. Also, during open market hours, option prices can fluctuate rapidly based on the price movement of the underlying stock.

2. If there are multiple stocks from the same industry segment, it will be best to use only one of them to avoid concentration risk.

Conclusion

Please carefully review the goals of each of the distinct strategies. We think these lists offer great selections for writing/selling put or call options. As usual, we have tried to put relatively safe stocks in both groups; however, the stocks listed in the second list for call options (or buy-write call options) have been specifically filtered based on the safety of their dividends. Please note that nothing is absolutely safe in the investing world.

data.title: Super Micro Computer: Overhyped AI Story With Underwhelming Delivery
data.description:
Heading into 2025, I argued for a "Sell" rating on Super Micro Computer (NASDAQ:SMCI), highlighting (i) negative operating cash flow, (ii) the risk for SMCI seeking additional capital, diluting shareholders, (iii) concerns over internal controls and board independence, as well as (iv) rich valuation multiples.

So far, shares have not yet acknowledged my arguments, with SMCI stock up nearly 30% YTD, while the S&P 500 (SP500) is down approximately 2%. But reflecting on SMCI's commercial momentum through the first few months of the year, I am gaining more confidence in my bearish assessment: Investors should note that both SMCI's Q3 FY 2025 results, as well as Q4 FY 2025 guidance, disappointed against consensus estimates on both topline and earnings. Meanwhile, CAPEX forecasts for AI infrastructure spending continue to trend up, suggesting that SMCI is struggling to capitalize on the AI infrastructure boom despite the company's (supposedly) leading positioning in the server market.

SMCI Commercial Momentum Disappoints Against Consensus Expectations ...

SMCI's Q3 FY 2025 results, announced on April 30, 2025, underscored operational challenges. The company reported revenue of $4.55 billion, falling short of consensus estimates of $5.25 billion and its own prior guidance of $5–$6 billion (consensus estimates based on Refinitiv data, estimated before the Q3 earnings warning). This miss was attributed to delays in AI server orders and supply chain bottlenecks. Notably, SMCI management has previously estimated that order delays could cost up to $1.4 billion. Earnings per share came in at $0.31, below the expected $0.55-0.6 (again, consensus estimates based on Refinitiv data, estimated before the Q3 earnings warning).

While initial hopes pointed to a swift rebound following the Q3 miss, supported by an understanding that one-off factors like customer platform decisions and inventory write-downs were major headwinds in the quarter, the path forward appears more complex. In fact, the Q4 FY 2025 outlook from Super Micro now suggests that these challenges are not fleeting. Customer deployment push-outs in Q3 were more widespread than initially thought, indicating ongoing difficulties as clients weigh multiple platform options and contend with datacenter readiness. This means the deferred Q3 FY 2025 revenue is unlikely to be fully captured in a single quarter (Q4 FY 2025), but rather will spread over several quarters. Furthermore, gross margin pressures persist, with lower-cost inventory issues still a factor, potentially impacting Q4 gross margins by up to 100 basis points and guiding towards a ~10% figure.

With that said, Q4 FY 2025 guidance is projecting revenue of $5.6-6.4 billion against analyst expectations of $6.9 billion, according to data collected by Refinitiv. Following Q3, FY 2025 revenue estimates dropped by about $3 billion since the beginning of the year, now at around $22 billion.

Similarly, FY 2025 EBIT has dropped aggressively, to $1.6 billion, compared to estimates of around $2.6 billion a year ago.

... While The AI Infrastructure Backdrop Is Still Booming

Early 2025 brought concerns about weakening demand for AI infrastructure chips. This impacting AI sentiment and contributing to a significant (temporary) share price decline in semiconductor stocks. These worries were underscored by SMCI's unexpected earnings update on April 30, 2025, when the company announced a $1.4 billion write-down and lowered its Q3 revenue guidance to $4.5–$4.6 billion from $5–$6 billion.

However, it is important to note that, despite these demand fears, underlying fundamentals remain robust: In fact, Q1 2025 earnings from major hyperscalers (Meta, Google, Microsoft, Amazon) showed no reduction in AI infrastructure CAPEX compared to prior projections. Even better, according to Evercore, 2025 CAPEX estimates for these hyperscalers have increased 25% YoY, with a 13% rise since November 2024 and a 2% increase since February 2025.

Source: Evercore research note on the Semiconductor industry, dated May 2, 2025.

Thus, as the AI infrastructure backdrop remains strong, there is an argument to be made that weakness in SMCI's Q3 results and Q4 guidance is company-specific. That would be a very bad read-through, if true. On that note, I highlight that estimates for Dell's infrastructure segment (FY 2025) have consistently trended upwards over the past 2 years, and estimates have remained strong despite the deployment push-outs noted by SMCI management.

Valuation: Shares Look Richly Valued

Focusing on a potential valuation estimate for SMCI, I structured a residual earnings model building on EPS estimates through CY 2027. Specifically, I estimate EPS at $2.1 for 2025, $2.8 for 2026, and $3.3 for 2027, while assuming an 11% cost of equity and a 3.25% terminal growth rate. These inputs yield a revised base case target price of ~$34.

Below, readers may also find the updated sensitivity table for varying assumptions on the cost of equity (rows) and terminal growth rates (columns).

Investor Takeaway

I maintain my bearish stance on SMCI and reiterate a "Sell" rating. Despite strong AI infrastructure tailwinds and a 30% YTD rally in the stock, SMCI's fundamentals have started to fracture meaningfully: Q3 FY 2025 results missed consensus by wide margins. At the same time, weak Q4 guidance suggested that commercial turbulence is not just limited to Q3, but that there is a risk for a deeper momentum crack caused by supply chain delays and customer deployment push-outs. More importantly, the company's inability to convert booming AI datacenter CAPEX into top-line strength—while peers like Dell continue to see upward estimate revisions—suggests SMCI's execution gap is widening.

Lastly, valuation remains a major concern. Based on my residual earnings model, which incorporates more realistic EPS assumptions and an 11% cost of equity, I derive a base case target of $34 per share, materially below current levels. As estimates reset lower and the market begins to digest SMCI's competitive and operational missteps, I expect shareholder optimism to fade. In my view, SMCI is an overhyped AI story with underwhelming delivery.

data.title: The Simple Path To Dividend Wealth (What I'm Buying)
data.description:
J.L. Collins' book The Simple Path To Wealth is one of those that I've never read, even though I feel like I've read it because of how familiar I am with the concepts of the book.

Collins compiles virtually all of the best, most commonsense wisdom on personal finance and combines it with a simple, low-cost, "get-rich-slow" investment method. Getting rich doesn't require genius. It just requires discipline and patience.

The principles of this path are probably familiar to you:

  • Regularly spend less than you earn and save the surplus. Don't allow lifestyle inflation to control your spending.
  • Aggressively avoid and/or pay off all forms of debt besides a long-term fixed-rate mortgage.
  • Invest your surplus cash into low-cost, passive index funds or ETFs.
  • Don't try to time the market or actively invest in individual stocks like a full-time portfolio manager when you aren't one. Even most full-time portfolio managers underperform their passive index benchmarks!
  • Eventually become more of a capitalist than a laborer and attain an "F-you" level of wealth that frees you from the need to work.
  • Use this financial freedom to design the life you want, do the work you want, and enjoy more of what makes you happy.

Discipline. Simplicity. Consistency.

That's the path to wealth and financial independence.

If I may be so bold, though, I dare say there are ways to slightly modify this path without degrading it and perhaps even upgrading it.

For example, dividend growth investors like me can easily modify the path by substituting a "net worth" view of wealth with a "cash flow" view of wealth. Thus, instead of needing to sell stocks to fund one's financial independence, one can live off the dividends without selling shares -- harvest the fruit while leaving the tree intact.

Lately, with fewer conspicuous buying opportunities on my radar, I've had more time to contemplate investment philosophy. Collins' framework is incredibly helpful, but I've tweaked it in a way that makes sense to me and would likely be beneficial to most individual investors.

Before I get to that subject, though, let me address a few timely topics, as I usually do in these weekly articles.

  • Why the International Trade court's strike-down of "Liberation Day" tariffs is good for the market in a Big Picture sense, despite some caveats and lingering uncertainties.
  • A reminder of why free international trade benefits both the economy and stock performance.
  • My thoughts on "the simple path to dividend wealth."
  • What this dividend growth investor is buying today.

Onward!

Judicial Trifecta Strikes Down Trump's Tariffs

By now, everyone should have heard about the three-member panel of trade court judges that struck down President Trump's "Liberation Day" tariffs as unconstitutional. If not, try the Wall Street Journal's reporting about it here.

The three judges -- one appointed by Reagan, another by Obama, and a third by Trump -- unanimously concluded that the President's legal justification to impose sweeping and open-ended tariffs on every nation across the globe (including some uninhabited ones) is unconstitutional.

Such tariffs would have to come from Congress, which the Constitution intended to control the nation's spending and revenue-raising.

In a very Big Picture sense, I think this judicial ruling is a critical brick in the wall holding back Trump's mercantilist "America First" economic agenda. There may be more tinkering around the edges of trade policy, but Trump's vision of balanced trade flows, a resurgence of domestic manufacturing employment, and hundreds of billions of dollars per year in tariff revenue now seems exceedingly unlikely to manifest.

While this judgment is gratifying for those of us who understand the benefits of international trade, some caveats should be noted.

First, trade uncertainty remains, and this judicial decision perhaps increases and extends the uncertainty. Trump can appeal the decision as well as seek a stay on it, and we don't know what the outcomes of those developments will be. Nor do we know how this decision will affect ongoing trade negotiations.

Quoting the WSJ:

The order blows a hole in global trade talks, already under way with more than a dozen nations, which began after the reciprocal tariffs were imposed. It also throws into question recent agreements with the U.K. and China.

Why would foreign countries want to sign a long-term trade agreement with the US now? Why not wait and see how the legal process plays out?

This is especially true of China, which has proven the resilience of its manufacturing sector in the face of disrupted trade with the US:

Second, there are other, albeit more limited, legal justifications that can be used to reimpose tariffs, as explained by a recent note from Goldman Sachs. Whether these other legal justifications can collectively replace the sweeping use of the "national emergency" justification is uncertain.

Third, sector-based tariffs for "national security," such as the steel & aluminum and auto tariffs, remain in place and were not struck down by the court. Tariffs on imported drugs and semiconductors could still be imposed at some point based on this other legal justification.

Fourth, how will this judgment affect already announced and implemented price hikes? For example, Walmart (WMT) has already raised prices of a large swathe of items. And the purveyor of "Everyday Low Prices" is far from alone. Lots of businesses and retailers have stated their intention to raise prices.

Reminder: International Trade Is Good

It's worth briefly commenting on why international trade and even a trade deficit is good for the United States and the vast majority of American workers and consumers.

In their excellent book Common Sense Economics, five economists summarize why the freedom of individuals and businesses to trade across borders is economically beneficial (p. 77-85).

  1. It promotes specialization of production across national economies, incentivizing each nation to focus on the highest and best use of their unique resources.
  2. It facilitates larger economies of scale, making businesses more efficient and consumer goods cheaper.
  3. It gives consumers and businesses access to a wider variety of goods.
  4. It promotes greater competition and efficiency among domestic producers.

On that first point, consider the fact that as low-margin, labor-intensive industries have waned in the US, high-margin and high-tech industries with high-paying jobs have surged.

As a country's real GDP per capita grows and technology advances, the portion of the workforce employed in the physical production of goods naturally declines over time.

This is the case in countries like the US that consistently run a trade deficit as well as countries like Germany that consistently run trade surpluses:

If you looked at agriculture employment from the middle of the 19th century to the middle of the 20th, you'd see the same trajectory. During this period, agricultural technology and productivity boomed, reducing the number of required workers. Those lost agriculture jobs were replaced by better jobs up the value chain of the economy.

The same happened with manufacturing.

And has this process been bad for America? Has it hollowed out the middle class? Has it replaced well-paid manufacturing jobs with low-paid service jobs?

No.

These populist narratives are totally contradicted by the data. For example, Census Bureau data shows that in inflation-adjusted dollars, both middle-income and low-income households have shrunk over the last half-century while high-income households make up a larger share of the total than ever before.

The economy may not be better in every way or for every person today relative to 20 or 30 or 40 years ago, but it is undoubtedly better, on net, for the population as a whole.

International trade has played a significant role in that progress.

And, as it turns out, while the idea that there should be more American manufacturing jobs is popular, only a minority of Americans actually want to work those jobs themselves.

The beauty of the four principles above is that they do not require complete reciprocity between countries. More freedom to trade is better, even if that freedom is being granted unilaterally.

Here's how Common Sense Economics puts it (p. 85):

We should present the case for free trade to other countries, but just because others are employing harmful policies, it does not follow that we should. To the contrary, the United States would gain substantially if it unilaterally phased out all of its trade restrictions over, for example, a ten-year period. Such an action would improve the well-being of Americans and, at the same time, improve economic conditions around the world.

And to quote Ronald Reagan (who was on a very different rhetorical plane than the current Republican president) from a 1988 radio address:

Our peaceful trading partners are not our enemies; they are our allies. We should beware of the demagogues who are ready to declare a trade war against our friends -- weakening our economy, our national security, and the entire free world -- all while cynically waving the American flag. The expansion of the international economy is not a foreign invasion; it is an American triumph, one we worked hard to achieve, and something central to our vision of a peaceful and prosperous world of freedom.

Here's the bottom line for investors: More international trade (imports and exports) is overwhelmingly a net positive for the economy.

More and freer trade is a boon to GDP growth, job security, consumer prices, and, yes, the stock market.

The Path To Wealth Is Paved With Dividends

Back to the "simple path to wealth."

Modifying The Path With Dividend Growth

Forgive my insolence, but I think modifying Collins' advice from dollar-cost averaging into low-cost all-stock funds like the Vanguard Total Stock Market ETF (VTI) or the Vanguard Total World Stock ETF (VT) to dollar-cost averaging into low-cost, broad-based dividend growth funds would be an improvement.

Moderately high-earners and/or highly frugal savers will eventually be able to live entirely off dividends. And for those who don't quite reach that point, there's still the possibility of living off a combination of dividends and realized gains (i.e. selling shares).

While the tax code may not favor dividends over long-term capital gains, there is an intuitive appeal to living off dividends rather than selling shares, if feasible.

Consider Warren Buffett's longstanding advice to think of stock investments as ownership stakes in real businesses. After all, that's what they are.
